title: God and My Enemies
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 1-5-25 -AM- God and My Enemies - Psalm 59 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Starting the Year Contemplating God
subpoints: The new year creates a psychological mindset for new beginnings — let us use it to contemplate God
Psalm 59: a maskil (teaching psalm) of David, written when Saul sent men to watch his house to kill him (1 Sam. 19:11-17)
Two categories: David's enemies and David's God
Aim: spend more time on David's God — who is also our God

outline.title: David's Enemies
subpoints: David's enemies are real — not paranoia (v. 1-2): he broadens his petition to all his enemies, making room for every child of God to stand with him
Our enemies: the world, the flesh, and the devil — always at enmity with the people of God (1 John 2:15-17; James 4:4)
The moment we forget we have enemies, we have already lost the battle
The enemies attack most mercilessly when one is blameless (v. 3-4): God will give victory
The enemies are proud and cocky: "Who hears?" — believing there is no God to listen (v. 6-7)
The enemies are persistent (v. 14-15): like a hungry wild dog roaming the streets
David's concern is not his own well-being but the dishonor enemies bring to God's name (v. 11-13)
Deliverance belongs to the Lord — David does not avenge himself (v. 4-5; Rom. 12:19-21)

outline.title: David's God
subpoints: God delivers His people from their enemies (v. 1-2; Gal. 1:3-5: Jesus gave Himself to deliver us from this present evil age)
God is David's God — a personal relationship, obtained through faith in Jesus Christ (Col. 1:13-14)
God is the Lord God of Hosts — the General of heaven's armies (Yahweh Sabaoth); 12 legions of angels available (Matt. 26:53); the chariots of fire that surrounded Elisha (2 Kings 6)
God is the God of Israel — the covenant-keeping God who says, "I will be your God and you will be my people," confirmed in Christ
God rules in Jacob — sovereign over all places, all situations, to the ends of the earth (v. 13)
God is our defense and shield — our fortress and refuge (v. 9, 11, 16, 17; Col. 3: hidden in Christ, doubly hidden in the Father)
God is powerful — holds the universe together by the word of His power; has the power to save and to keep saved (1 Pet. 1:5)
God is merciful — hesed: steadfast love, covenant faithfulness, loving-kindness (v. 10, 16, 17; Lam. 3:22-23)

outline.title: Application: Starting 2025 With God
subpoints: Homework: re-read Psalm 59 at home and underline everything it says about God
Think of your enemies — those who are truly against you because of the gospel
Turn back to David's God — who is your God through faith in Jesus Christ
Set your goal for 2025: know your God better, grow in your relationship with Him, make Him known
Follow David's example in verse 16: sing of God's power and mercy in the morning

summary: On the first Sunday of 2025, Pastor Tito Lyro uses Psalm 59 — David's song written when Saul sent men to watch his house to kill him — to lead the congregation in starting the new year by contemplating God. Organizing the Psalm into two categories, David's enemies and David's God, he first establishes that David's enemies are real, vicious, proud, and persistent — and that as believers, our enemies are the world, the flesh, and the devil, which never cease their assault. The sermon's greater focus is on David's God: the God who delivers (Gal. 1:3-5), the God who is personally known through faith in Christ (Col. 1:13-14), the Lord of hosts commanding heaven's armies (2 Kings 6), the covenant-keeping God who will never leave or forsake His people, the sovereign ruler of Jacob to the ends of the earth, the unassailable fortress and refuge, the one whose power holds the universe together and keeps believers saved (1 Pet. 1:5), and the God of hesed — steadfast covenant mercy that is new every morning (Lam. 3:22-23). The sermon closes with a call to start 2025 by growing in the knowledge and proclamation of this God.
transcript:
## Psalm 59

Today is the first Sunday of the month, and it is good for us to start the year contemplating God. Psalm 59 helps us do exactly that. Let us read it together:

"To the Chief Musician. Set to 'Do Not Destroy.' A Michtam of David when Saul sent men, and they watched the house in order to kill him. Deliver me from my enemies, O my God; defend me from those who rise up against me. Deliver me from the workers of iniquity, and save me from bloodthirsty men. For look, they lie in wait for my life; the mighty gather against me, not for my transgression nor for my sin, O Lord. They run and prepare themselves through no fault of mine. Awake to help me, and behold! You therefore, O Lord God of hosts, the God of Israel, awake to punish all the nations; do not be merciful to any wicked transgressors. At evening they return, they growl like a dog, and go all around the city. Indeed, they belch with their mouth; swords are in their lips, for they say, 'Who hears?' But You, O Lord, shall laugh at them; You shall have all the nations in derision. I will wait for You, O You his strength; for God is my defense. My God of mercy shall come to meet me; God shall let me see my desire on my enemies. Do not slay them, lest my people forget; scatter them by Your power, and bring them down, O Lord our shield. For the sin of their mouth and the words of their lips, let them even be taken in their pride, and for the cursing and lying which they speak. Consume them in wrath, consume them, that they may not be; and let them know that God rules in Jacob to the ends of the earth. And at evening they return, they growl like a dog, and go all around the city. They wander up and down for food, and howl if they are not satisfied. But I will sing of Your power; yes, I will sing aloud of Your mercy in the morning; for You have been my defense and refuge in the day of my trouble. To You, O my Strength, I will sing praises; for God is my defense, my God of mercy."

## Introduction: Starting the Year With God

It is good for us to start the year contemplating God. Costco knows that in January people buy bins to organize their homes, exercise equipment, and furniture — because people commit to working out, organizing their houses, and buying new furniture. And what usually happens? The workout equipment becomes something to organize clothes on, and people sit on the new furniture watching TV. But let us make a different commitment: to start this year by contemplating who God is.

Psalm 59 may seem like a strange passage for this — it seems to be about enemies and wrath. But I hope to show you how it helps us contemplate God. This is a maskil — a teaching psalm — whose title tells us exactly what prompted it: Saul sent men to watch David's house in order to kill him (1 Samuel 19:11-17). David is thinking back on this experience after the fact and writing for the benefit of God's people.

We will divide the Psalm into two categories: **David's enemies** and **David's God**. We will aim to spend more time on David's God — because David's God is also our God.

## David's Enemies

**David's enemies are real.** He is not paranoid. He has enemies he can point to — the men coming to his house. But notice that in verses 1 and 2, he broadens his petition beyond the immediate situation to include all his enemies. He makes room for us. Every child of God who has been afflicted by enemies can stand with David in this prayer.

The enemies we face may not be men sitting outside our window — but brothers and sisters, the world, the flesh, and the devil are always at enmity with us. 1 John 2:15-17: "Do not love the world or the things in the world. If anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him." James 4:4: "Friendship with the world is enmity with God." There is no gray here — either you are a friend of God or a friend of the enemy. The moment we forget we have enemies, we have lost the battle.

**The enemies attack even when one is blameless** (v. 3-4). David says he has not sinned against them, yet they come. The enemy will invest most in destroying those who are honoring the name of Christ.

**The enemies are proud and cocky** (v. 6-7). They say, "Who hears?" — meaning, there is no God who can hear and respond. That is the lie the enemy will whisper to you: there is no one to hear your prayers. But God promised: "I will never leave you nor forsake you."

**The enemies are persistent** (v. 14-15): like a hungry wild dog roaming the streets, ready to attack anyone.

**David's deepest concern is not his own safety but God's honor** (v. 11-13). He asks God to deal with his enemies in a way that makes clear who God is. We like to justify our anger as righteous anger — but I wonder how much of it is because of dishonor to God's name versus dishonor to our little personal kingdoms.

**Deliverance belongs to the Lord.** David does not avenge himself. Romans 12:19-21: "Beloved, do not avenge yourselves... 'Vengeance is Mine, I will repay,' says the Lord. Therefore if your enemy is hungry, feed him." This is a freeing thought — you do not have to vindicate yourself. You do not have to correct everything people say about you. God will do it. You can go around loving people and not worrying about your reputation.

## David's God

**God delivers His people from their enemies.** Galatians 1:3-5: Jesus gave Himself for our sins, that He might deliver us from this present evil age. All of us will be delivered from this present evil age — on God's timeline, which is the best timeline.

**God is David's personal God.** In verse 1, David calls Him "my God." This is not merely Jesse's God or Israel's God — He is David's God. How does God become my God? Through faith in the Lord Jesus Christ. Colossians 1:13-14: "He has delivered us from the dominion of darkness and transferred us to the kingdom of His beloved Son, in whom we have redemption through His blood, the forgiveness of sins."

**God is the Lord God of Hosts** (v. 5). This is the name Yahweh Sabaoth — the General of heaven's armies. When Peter took out his sword in Gethsemane, Jesus said: "Do you think that I cannot now pray to My Father, and He will provide Me with more than twelve legions of angels?" When Elisha's servant was terrified by the Syrian army, Elisha prayed: "Lord, open his eyes." And the servant saw that the mountain was full of horses and chariots of fire. Hebrews 1:14 tells us those angels are ministering servants to those who believe in Jesus Christ. That is our God.

**God is the God of Israel** — the covenant-keeping God. He promised: "I will be your God and you will be My people." Jesus is the final revelation of that promise. God is our God and we are His people — in 2025 and through all eternity.

**God rules in Jacob to the ends of the earth** (v. 13). There is nowhere you go as a child of God where God is not the sovereign ruler. Antarctica, the Arctic, the Sahara, Timbuktu — He is sovereign over that place and that situation.

**God is our defense and shield** (v. 9, 11, 16, 17). Our high tower, our fortress, our refuge. We all have our thin little shields that we trust — whatever we think will protect us. But anything that is not God as our protection is like a thin sheet on a hot night. Paul says in Colossians 3 that we are hidden in God in Christ — doubly hidden. Christ's hand is around us, and the Father's hand is around Christ. We cannot be plucked out.

**God is powerful.** The complexity of the universe — 10 to the 18th power atoms, all kept together — and the Scripture says it is held together by the word of our Savior. Just by speaking, all things are held together. He has the power to save sinners, and He has the power to keep us saved. Peter says we are kept by the power of God through faith (1 Pet. 1:5). We do not walk away from the Lord because He is keeping us.

**God is merciful** (v. 10, 16, 17). The word hesed — mercy, goodness, kindness, steadfast love, loyalty, covenant faithfulness, loving-kindness. It is all these things. And He is that because He promised to be that for us. Lamentations 3:22-23: Jeremiah, standing on the rubble of Jerusalem, says: "Through the Lord's mercies we are not consumed, because His compassions fail not. They are new every morning. Great is Your faithfulness." He said that standing in the ruins. We deserve worse than what we have — and it is only by God's mercy that we are still here.

## Application: Starting 2025 With God

Your homework: when you go home today, read Psalm 59 again. Underline everything it says concerning God. Think about each description. Think of your enemies — those who are truly against you because of the gospel. And then turn back to David's God — your God — who you know through faith in Jesus Christ.

Set your goal for 2025: know your God better. Grow in your relationship with Him. Make Him known.

Do what David did in verse 16: "But I will sing of Your power; yes, I will sing aloud of Your mercy in the morning; for You have been my defense and refuge in the day of my trouble." That is our God, brothers and sisters. May He be praised in our hearts.
